_meta.title: Synchronization In The Kernel Spinlocks Rcu

questions.question: Why are spinlocks preferred over mutexes for very short critical sections in the kernel?
options: Spinlocks use less memory than mutexes
The spin-wait overhead is less than the context switch cost required by a sleeping mutex
Spinlocks can be held across interrupt handlers without additional measures
Mutexes cannot protect shared data in the kernel
questions.explanation: A mutex puts the waiting thread to sleep, requiring a context switch on lock and unlock. If the critical section is shorter than the context switch cost (nanoseconds to low microseconds), spinning wastes less time than sleeping.

questions.question: In RCU (Read-Copy-Update), when is the old version of data freed?
options: Immediately after the writer publishes the new pointer
After the next context switch on every CPU
After a grace period during which all pre-existing readers have finished
When the writer calls kfree() before publishing
questions.explanation: RCU defers freeing the old data until all readers that may have obtained a reference to it have exited their read-side critical sections. This grace period is detected when every CPU has passed through at least one quiescent state (context switch, idle loop, or user mode).

questions.question: What happens when a seqlock reader detects that a write was in progress or occurred during its read?
options: The reader blocks until the writer finishes
The reader acquires the writer's lock and retries
The reader discards its data and retries the read from the beginning
The reader returns stale data with a warning flag
questions.explanation: Seqlock readers never block or acquire locks. They check the sequence counter: if it is odd (write in progress) or has changed since they started (write completed during read), they simply discard whatever they read and retry.

questions.question: What problem do memory barriers (`smp_mb()`, `smp_rmb()`, `smp_wmb()`) solve?
options: They prevent cache coherency protocol failures
They prevent the CPU and compiler from reordering memory operations across the barrier, ensuring ordering guarantees for lock-free code
They flush the entire CPU cache to main memory
They disable interrupts on the local CPU
questions.explanation: Modern CPUs and compilers reorder loads and stores for performance. Without memory barriers, a pointer might become visible to another CPU before the object it points to is fully initialized. Memory barriers enforce ordering so that lock-free algorithms see consistent state.

questions.question: Why can't the kernel use regular mutexes in interrupt handlers?
options: Interrupt handlers don't need synchronization
Interrupt handlers can't sleep (they run in interrupt context), so only spinlocks or lock-free mechanisms work
Mutexes are too slow for the kernel
Interrupt handlers always run on a separate CPU
questions.explanation: A mutex may put the calling thread to sleep while waiting. Interrupt handlers run in a special context where sleeping is forbidden — there's no scheduler to wake them up. Spinlocks busy-wait instead of sleeping, making them suitable for interrupt context and very short critical sections.
